text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review and Subsequent Office Assessment

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]
SERVICE: Genitourinary Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient Follow-Up / Post-Tumor Board Counseling

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with germline BRCA2 mutation, now presenting for follow-up after emergency restaging imaging confirming visceral progression (hepatic and pulmonary metastases) and pathologic evidence of treatment-emergent neuroendocrine differentiation on liver biopsy. The patient was presented at our GU Multidisciplinary Tumor Board earlier this week. He is here today to discuss the tumor board recommendations, review biopsy and molecular results in detail, and finalize next steps in his treatment plan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old retired steelworker with a well-documented history of stage IV metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago. He carries a germline BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22), consistent with a hereditary breast/ovarian cancer syndrome pedigree (brother with pancreatic cancer, sister with BRCA2-associated breast cancer). His initial Gleason 9 (4+5) adenocarcinoma presented with multifocal bone metastases (lumbar spine, right iliac wing, right femoral neck) and right obturator lymph node involvement, with a presenting PSA of 187 ng/mL.

He was initially treated with ADT (leuprolide) plus abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) with prednisone for mHSPC intensification, achieving a PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL and radiographic improvement. He developed CRPC approximately 2 years after diagnosis with PSA rise to 4.7 ng/mL on 3 consecutive measurements and new bone metastases on PSMA PET/CT. He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily with an initial PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL, but progressed after approximately 6 months with rising PSA to 8.3 ng/mL and substantial new osseous metastases on repeat PSMA PET/CT. A confirmatory CT-guided right iliac crest biopsy at that time showed metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with biallelic BRCA2 loss confirmed by FISH, no neuroendocrine differentiation, Ki-67 35%.

Given his germline and somatic biallelic BRCA2 loss and PROfound trial data supporting olaparib (Lynparza) in this setting, he was initiated on olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily approximately 9 months ago. He had a meaningful initial response with PSA declining from 8.3 to a nadir of 2.1 ng/mL and radiographic improvement in osseous and nodal disease at 5-month restaging. His course was complicated by recurrent olaparib-related myelosuppression, requiring two separate packed red blood cell transfusions (the first at 3 months, the second at 6 months) and olaparib dose reduction to 200 mg twice daily following the first transfusion for Grade 3 anemia (Hgb nadir 7.6 g/dL). He also received IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) on two occasions and hematology was co-managing his anemia.

Approximately 3 weeks ago, he presented with new right upper quadrant discomfort, 6 lb unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks, and a PSA that had risen precipitously from the 2.1 nadir to 14.6 ng/mL. Physical exam revealed mild hepatomegaly. Urgent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast demonstrated new hepatic metastases (up to 2.8 cm in the right lobe, with a smaller left lobe lesion), new bilateral pulmonary nodules concerning for pulmonary metastases, progression of right obturator lymph node (3.1 cm) with new left external iliac adenopathy, and further progressive osseous disease. This represented clear visceral spread and olaparib resistance.

A CT-guided liver biopsy was performed and returned showing metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with focal neuroendocrine differentiation — synaptophysin focally positive in approximately 10-15% of tumor cells and chromogranin A focally positive — raising concern for treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC), a well-recognized phenomenon in this heavily pre-treated, AR-pathway-driven disease context. Ki-67 proliferation index was markedly elevated at 55%. PSA and NKX3.1 were retained, confirming the prostatic origin. AR expression was moderate (2+) rather than absent, suggesting an early or partial neuroendocrine transdifferentiation rather than fully dedifferentiated small cell NE carcinoma. Repeat somatic NGS on the liver biopsy specimen identified newly acquired AR amplification (copy number 12) and AR LBD mutation p.L702H, MYC amplification (now copy number 18, up from 6 at diagnosis), RB1 biallelic loss (new from prior), CDK4 amplification, CCND1 gain, and a newly detected PIK3CA p.H1047R mutation — a molecular landscape reflective of treatment-driven clonal evolution and aggressive disease biology. No BRCA2 reversion mutations were identified, confirming that PARP inhibitor resistance is not mediated by BRCA2 restoration in this case.

The case was reviewed at GU Multidisciplinary Tumor Board, attended by GU medical oncology, radiation oncology, urology, palliative care, and diagnostic radiology. The consensus recommendation was to discontinue olaparib and pursue platinum-based chemotherapy with carboplatin/etoposide, given the neuroendocrine features on histology, the heavily pre-treated state, and the established role of platinum doublets in t-NEPC. Cabazitaxel was also discussed but felt to be less optimal in the setting of emerging NE differentiation. Radiation for palliation of symptomatic osseous sites can be considered as needed. Formal integration of palliative care was strongly recommended.

Mr. Kowalski is here today with his daughter, who is his primary caregiver. He has been told the biopsy showed an aggressive change in his cancer. He comes in understandably anxious but appears engaged. He states he wants to understand his options and has expressed a clear preference to pursue one additional line of treatment, acknowledging that his disease is incurable. He does not wish to discuss hospice at this time but is open to palliative care support.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Ongoing fatigue (worse over past 3-4 weeks, Grade 2-3), 6 lb unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks, decreased appetite, intermittent night sweats
- Gastrointestinal: Right upper quadrant discomfort (dull, non-radiating, worse with palpation), mild nausea without vomiting, no diarrhea, no jaundice
- Musculoskeletal: Persistent low back pain (L3-L4 region, Grade 2, partially controlled with scheduled acetaminophen and occasional oxycodone), new right hip ache (Grade 1-2), no pathologic fracture
- Pulmonary: Mild dyspnea on exertion (Grade 1-2), no hemoptysis, no cough
- Genitourinary: Urinary function stable, no gross hematuria
- Neurological: No new neurological deficits, no signs of spinal cord compromise, no headache, no confusion
- Hematologic: Ongoing fatigue and dyspnea attributed in part to anemia; last transfusion approximately 3 months ago
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Hot flashes persist (ADT-related, Grade 1), no clinical signs of adrenal insufficiency
- Psychiatric: Anxiety related to new disease trajectory, functional coping with daughter's support

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 37.0°C, oxygen saturation 95% on room air, weight 172 lb (down from 178 lb at prior visit), height 5'10", ECOG performance status 2-3.

General: Elderly male, appears fatigued and mildly cachectic. Alert and oriented x3. Somewhat anxious but cooperative. Accompanied by daughter.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Anicteric sclerae. Mild conjunctival pallor consistent with anemia. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable.

Chest/Respiratory: Breath sounds clear to auscultation bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. No dullness to percussion.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant. Liver edge palpable approximately 3 cm below the right costal margin — mild hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No ascites. No rebound tenderness or guarding.

Musculoskeletal: Mild tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine (L3-L4 region). Antalgic gait favoring left leg due to right hip discomfort. No gross bony deformity. Strength 4/5 in bilateral lower extremities, symmetric.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No signs of epidural spinal cord compression (preserved rectal tone per prior exam, no saddle anesthesia reported, no new lower extremity weakness suggesting cauda equina syndrome — patient advised to call immediately if any change).

Skin: No rash. No jaundice. No palmar erythema.

Lymph Nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or palpable pelvic lymphadenopathy on external exam.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
PSA: 18.4 ng/mL (up from nadir of 2.1 ng/mL; rapid rise over approximately 8-9 weeks)
Testosterone: < 20 ng/dL (castrate state maintained on leuprolide)
Chromogranin A: 142 ng/mL (elevated; upper limit of normal approximately 100 ng/mL at our institution) — consistent with neuroendocrine activity
Neuron-specific enolase (NSE): 28 U/L (elevated; upper limit of normal approximately 17 U/L) — further supporting neuroendocrine differentiation
LDH: 318 U/L (elevated)
Alkaline phosphatase: 214 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone and hepatic involvement)
AST: 52 U/L (mildly elevated)
ALT: 48 U/L (mildly elevated)
Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (normal)
Albumin: 3.2 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with nutritional compromise)
Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (stable, eGFR approximately 65 mL/min/1.73m²)
Hemoglobin: 8.9 g/dL (Grade 2-3 anemia; was 10.1 post-transfusion ~3 months ago — trending down again)
Platelets: 148 K/uL (low-normal, likely persistent olaparib-related myelosuppression)
WBC: 4.1 K/uL (low-normal)
ANC: 2.2 K/uL (adequate, no frank neutropenia)
Calcium: 9.8 mg/dL (normal)
Phosphorus: 3.6 mg/dL (normal)
Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L (normal)
Glucose: 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with known T2DM, well-controlled)
HbA1c: 7.1% (last checked, stable)

---

IMAGING RESULTS (REVIEWED):
Recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (from approximately 3 weeks ago) reviewed in detail with radiology at tumor board. Findings discussed as follows:

Hepatic: Three discrete lesions in the right hepatic lobe measuring up to 2.8 cm, plus a left lobe lesion measuring 1.4 cm, all hypodense on portal venous phase with arterial phase enhancement pattern consistent with hepatic metastatic disease. No biliary dilation. Hepatomegaly with liver spanning approximately 18 cm in the craniocaudal dimension.

Pulmonary: A right lower lobe pulmonary nodule measuring 0.9 cm and a left upper lobe nodule measuring 0.7 cm, both new compared with the restaging scan 5 months ago. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal adenopathy.

Nodal: The right obturator lymph node has grown to 3.1 cm (up from 1.2 cm at last assessment). A new left external iliac lymph node measures 1.8 cm. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric adenopathy of note.

Osseous: Diffuse osseous metastatic disease, with multiple areas of mixed lytic and sclerotic change. Previously identified lesions at L1-L4, sacrum, right iliac wing, femoral neck, ribs, sternum appear larger or more numerous. No pathologic fracture identified on available imaging. Neurosurgery/spine service already notified regarding lumbar spine involvement — no epidural extension on recent CT; MRI spine deferred pending clinical priority discussion.

Liver biopsy pathology and repeat NGS reviewed above (see HPI).

---

ASSESSMENT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with:

1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9, now with:
   - Visceral metastases to liver (4 lesions, up to 2.8 cm) and lungs (bilateral nodules)
   - Progressive nodal disease (right obturator 3.1 cm, new left external iliac 1.8 cm)
   - Widespread progressive osseous metastatic disease
   - Treatment-emergent neuroendocrine differentiation on liver biopsy (focal synaptophysin/chromogranin A positivity, Ki-67 55%, elevated serum chromogranin A 142 ng/mL and NSE 28 U/L)
   - Acquired AR amplification (copy number 12) and AR LBD p.L702H mutation, RB1 biallelic loss (newly acquired), MYC amplification, CDK4 amplification, PIK3CA H1047R — collectively reflecting marked molecular progression
   - No BRCA2 reversion mutation — PARP inhibitor resistance is not BRCA2-mediated
   - Disease progression on: ADT+abiraterone (CRPC development), enzalutamide (progression at 6 months), olaparib (progression at ~8-9 months)
   - ECOG performance status 2-3
   - Germline BRCA2 c.5946delT (pathogenic), consistent with hereditary cancer syndrome

2. Recurrent symptomatic anemia (Hgb 8.9 g/dL), multifactorial:
   - Ongoing olaparib-related myelosuppression (to be resolved with discontinuation)
   - Possible bone marrow infiltration given extensive osseous metastatic disease
   - Nutritional compromise (low albumin, borderline iron stores)

3. Ongoing bone pain, partially controlled, with right hip and lumbar spine as predominant sites

4. Hypertension — appearing adequately controlled on current antihypertensive regimen

5. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled (HbA1c 7.1%)

6. Hyperlipidemia — on statin therapy

7. Goals of care: Patient has clearly expressed desire to pursue one additional line of active therapy with full understanding of the palliative intent. Hospice discussion deferred at patient request. Daughter (primary caregiver) in agreement. Palliative care to be formally integrated.

---

PLAN:

1. DISCONTINUE OLAPARIB:
   - Olaparib 200 mg twice daily is discontinued effective today given confirmed visceral and systemic progression with histologic and molecular evidence of resistance. No BRCA2 reversion mutation makes PARP inhibitor re-challenge non-indicated.

2. NEW SYSTEMIC THERAPY — CARBOPLATIN/ETOPOSIDE:
   - Following multidisciplinary tumor board recommendation, patient will be initiated on carboplatin AUC 5 IV on Day 1 plus etoposide 100 mg/m2 IV on Days 1, 2, and 3 every 21 days. Regimen chosen to address the treatment-emergent neuroendocrine component, as in small cell/NE prostate cancer and consistent with published data for t-NEPC (Aparicio et al.; Beltran et al.).
   - Treatment planning labs (CBC, CMP, creatinine-based carboplatin dose calculation using Calvert formula) to be obtained prior to Cycle 1.
   - Cycle 1 target start date within the next 7-10 days pending labs and patient readiness.
   - Pre-chemotherapy hydration and anti-emetic protocol will be employed (ondansetron, dexamethasone pre-treatment; lorazepam as needed for anticipatory nausea).
   - Filgrastim (G-CSF) prophylaxis discussed — given his baseline anemia, low-normal counts, and multiple prior lines of therapy, we will plan G-CSF support (pegfilgrastim on day 4 of each cycle) to reduce febrile neutropenia risk.
   - Patient verbalized understanding of expected side effects including myelosuppression (especially anemia, which is already present), nausea/vomiting, alopecia, fatigue, peripheral neuropathy, and infection risk.

3. CONTINUE ADT:
   - Leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months continued. Next injection due in approximately 6 weeks. Continue castrate testosterone suppression for maintained hormonal control of the AR-positive component.

4. CONTINUE DENOSUMAB (XGEVA):
   - Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks continued for skeletal-related event prevention given extensive bone metastatic disease. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation continue. Monitor renal function and electrolytes. Dental hygiene reminder documented — patient last saw dentist 6 months ago; avoid invasive dental procedures if possible.

5. ANEMIA MANAGEMENT:
   - Current Hgb 8.9 g/dL. With olaparib discontinuation, we anticipate some recovery in marrow function, though bone marrow infiltration cannot be excluded.
   - Repeat CBC in 2 weeks. Transfusion threshold: Hgb < 8 g/dL or symptomatic at any level. Patient instructed on symptoms requiring urgent attention.
   - Bone marrow biopsy deferred at this time — clinical picture is consistent with multifactorial anemia; proceed with carboplatin/etoposide and reassess hematologic parameters over next 4-6 weeks.
   - Hematology co-management continues. Dr. [Hematologist] notified of olaparib discontinuation and upcoming chemotherapy.

6. PAIN MANAGEMENT:
   - Current pain management: scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg every 8 hours plus as-needed oxycodone 5 mg for breakthrough pain.
   - Reassess pain control at next visit. If pain worsens, would escalate to scheduled low-dose long-acting opioid with breakthrough dosing. Palliative care will assist in pain optimization.
   - Spinal cord compression precautions discussed with patient — he has been thoroughly counseled on symptoms requiring emergency evaluation (new lower extremity weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, rapidly worsening back pain). Written instructions provided.
   - Radiation oncology referral placed for palliative radiation to the right hip/femoral neck region and/or lumbar spine if pain becomes refractory to pharmacologic management. This can be coordinated with the chemotherapy schedule.

7. PALLIATIVE CARE INTEGRATION:
   - Formal palliative care consultation requested and accepted today. Palliative care will be co-managing symptom burden including pain, fatigue, anorexia/weight loss, and anxiety going forward.
   - Social work consult also placed for caregiver support resources for the patient's daughter.
   - Advance care planning: Patient has a completed healthcare proxy designating his daughter. POLST form will be reviewed and updated with palliative care team. DNR/DNI discussion deferred per patient's wishes; he states he wants to revisit after beginning next treatment.

8. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT:
   - Albumin 3.2 g/dL, weight loss of 6 lb over 6 weeks — nutritional compromise is present and has implications for chemotherapy tolerance.
   - Dietary consult placed. Encourage high-protein, high-calorie diet. Appetite stimulant (mirtazapine 7.5 mg at bedtime) considered — initiated today, with additional benefit of anxiolytic effect.
   - Oral nutritional supplement (e.g., Ensure Plus) recommended twice daily.

9. MOLECULAR AND CLINICAL TRIAL DISCUSSION:
   - The newly detected PIK3CA H1047R mutation is potentially actionable. No PI3K/mTOR inhibitor is currently FDA-approved for mCRPC in this context, but clinical trials are available. Patient interested in clinical trial options after assessing carboplatin/etoposide tolerance. Will review available protocols at our institution.
   - NTRK, ALK, RET, FGFR and other targetable alterations not detected on the NGS panel.
   - No tumor mutational burden or microsatellite instability profile that would support checkpoint immunotherapy.

10. FOLLOW-UP:
    - Telephone check-in with RN in 48-72 hours to assess for worsening symptoms and confirm chemotherapy readiness.
    - Labs (CBC, CMP, Mg, phosphorus) in 10-14 days prior to Cycle 1 Day 1.
    - Next in-person oncology visit: Day 1 of Cycle 1 for treatment administration and pre-chemo assessment.
    - Repeat imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) planned after 2 cycles of carboplatin/etoposide to assess initial response.
    - PSA, chromogranin A, NSE, and LFTs to be followed at each cycle to monitor treatment response and hepatic function.

Time spent on this visit: Approximately 75 minutes, including review of tumor board recommendation, detailed counseling of patient and daughter, coordination of care, and documentation. More than 50% of visit time devoted to counseling and coordination.

Patient and caregiver verbalized understanding of the plan, risks, benefits, and alternatives. Questions were answered. Patient expressed appreciation for thorough communication and commitment to quality of life alongside disease management.

[Electronically signed — Attending Hematology/Oncology Physician]
[GU Medical Oncology]
[Copied to: Palliative Care, Hematology, Radiation Oncology, Primary Care Physician, Pharmacy]
